name: django-dynamic-preferences

metadata.name: django-dynamic-preferences
metadata.summary: Dynamic global and instance settings for your django project

documentation:
# django-dynamic-preferences

```{image} https://badge.fury.io/py/django-dynamic-preferences.png
:target: https://badge.fury.io/py/django-dynamic-preferences
```

```{image} https://readthedocs.org/projects/django-dynamic-preferences/badge/?version=latest
:target: http://django-dynamic-preferences.readthedocs.org/en/latest/
```

```{image} https://github.com/agateblue/django-dynamic-preferences/actions/workflows/tests.yml/badge.svg
:target: https://github.com/agateblue/django-dynamic-preferences/actions/workflows/tests.yml
```

```{image} https://opencollective.com/django-dynamic-preferences/backers/badge.svg
:alt: Backers on Open Collective
:target: '#backers'
```

```{image} https://opencollective.com/django-dynamic-preferences/sponsors/badge.svg
:alt: Sponsors on Open Collective
:target: '#sponsors'
```

Dynamic-preferences is a Django app, BSD-licensed, designed to help you manage your project settings. While most of the time,
a `settings.py` file is sufficient, there are some situations where you need something more flexible such as:

- per-user settings (or, generally speaking, per instance settings)
- settings change without server restart

For per-instance settings, you could actually store them in some kind of profile model. However, it means that every time you want to add a new setting, you need to add a new column to the profile DB table. Not very efficient.

Dynamic-preferences allow you to register settings (a.k.a. preferences) in a declarative way. Preferences values are serialized before storage in database, and automatically deserialized when you need them.

With dynamic-preferences, you can update settings on the fly, through django's admin or custom forms, without restarting your application.

The project is tested and work under Python 3.7, 3.8, 3.9, 3.10 and 3.11 and with django 3.2 and 4.2.

## Features

- Simple to setup
- Admin integration
- Forms integration
- Bundled with global and per-user preferences
- Can be extended to other models if need (e.g. per-site preferences)
- Integrates with django caching mechanisms to improve performance

## Documentation

The full documentation is at <https://django-dynamic-preferences.readthedocs.org>.

## Changelog

See <https://django-dynamic-preferences.readthedocs.io/en/latest/history.html>

## Contributing

See <https://django-dynamic-preferences.readthedocs.org/en/latest/contributing.html>

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.views.PreferenceFormView
documentation:
Display a form for updating preferences of the given
section provided via URL arg.
If no section is provided, will display a form for all
fields of a given registry.

modules.name: dynamic_preferences.preferences
documentation:
Preferences are regular Python objects that can be declared within any django app.
Once declared and registered, they can be edited by admins (for {py:class}`SitePreference` and {py:class}`GlobalPreference`)
and regular Users (for {py:class}`UserPreference`)

UserPreference, SitePreference and GlobalPreference are mapped to corresponding PreferenceModel,
which store the actual values.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.preferences.AbstractPreference

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.preferences.AbstractPreference.identifier
documentation: Return the name and the section of the Preference joined with a separator, with the form `section<separator>name`
A base class that handle common logic for preferences

functions.name: dynamic_preferences.processors.global_preferences
documentation:
Pass the values of global preferences to template context.
You can then access value with `global_preferences.<section>.<name>`

In some situations, such as with FileSerializer,

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.serializers.FakeInstance
documentation:
FieldFile needs a model instance to update when file is persisted
or deleted
FieldFile also needs a model instance to save changes.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.serializers.FakeField
documentation:
FieldFile needs a field object to generate a filename, persist
and delete files, so we are effectively mocking that.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.serializers.PreferenceFieldFile
documentation:
In order to have the same API that we have with models.FileField,
we must return a FieldFile object. However, there are various
things we have to override, since our files are not bound to a model
field.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.serializers.FileSerializer
documentation:
Since this serializer requires additional data from the preference
especially the upload path, we cannot do it without binding it
to the preference

it is therefore designed to be explicitely instanciated by the preference
object.

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.serializers.DateTimeSerializer.enforce_timezone
documentation:
When `self.default_timezone` is `None`, always return naive datetimes.
When `self.default_timezone` is not `None`, always return aware datetimes.

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.api.viewsets.PreferenceViewSet.bulk
documentation:
Update multiple preferences at once

this is a long method because we ensure everything is valid
before actually persisting the changes

- list preferences
- detail given preference
- batch update preferences
- update a single preference

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.settings.PreferenceSettings
documentation:
A settings object, that allows API settings to be accessed as properties.
For example:

    from rest_framework.settings import api_settings
    print(api_settings.DEFAULT_RENDERER_CLASSES)

Any setting with string import paths will be automatically resolved
and return the class, rather than the string literal.

documentation:
You'll find here the final, concrete classes of preferences you can use
in your own project.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType.get_initial
documentation:
:return:
    initial data for form field
    from field_attribute['initial'] or default

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType.field
documentation:
:return:
    an instance of a form field for this preference, with
    the correct configuration (widget, initial value, validators...)

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType.get_field_kwargs
documentation:
Return a dict of arguments to use as parameters for the field
class instianciation.

This will use {py:attr}`field_kwargs` as a starter,
and use sensible defaults for a few attributes:

- {py:attr}`instance.verbose_name` for the field label
- {py:attr}`instance.help_text` for the field help text
- {py:attr}`instance.widget` for the field widget
- {py:attr}`instance.required` defined if the value is required or not
- {py:attr}`instance.initial` defined if the initial value

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType.api_repr
documentation: Used only to represent a preference value using Rest Framework

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType.get_api_additional_data
documentation: Additional data to serialize for use on front-end side, for example

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType.get_api_field_data
documentation:
Field data to serialize for use on front-end side, for example
will include choices available for a choice field

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType.validate
documentation:
Used to implement custom cleaning logic for use in forms
and serializers. The method will be passed as a validator to
the preference form field.

```
Example

```

```python
def validate(self, value):
    if value == '42':
        raise ValidationError('Wrong value!')
```

class_variables.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType.field_class
documentation:
A form field that will be used to display and edit the preference
use a class, not an instance.

```
Example

```

```python
from django import forms

class MyPreferenceType(BasePreferenceType):
    field_class = forms.CharField
```

class_variables.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BasePreferenceType.field_kwargs
documentation:
Additional kwargs to be passed to the form field.

```
Example

```

```python
class MyPreference(StringPreference):

    field_kwargs = {
        'required': False,
        'initial': 'Hello there'
    }
```

Used as a base for all other preference classes. You should subclass
this one if you want to implement your own preference.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.BooleanPreference
documentation: A preference type that stores a boolean.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.IntegerPreference
documentation: A preference type that stores an integer.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.DecimalPreference
documentation: A preference type that stores a {py:class}`decimal.Decimal`.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.FloatPreference
documentation: A preference type that stores a float.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.StringPreference
documentation: A preference type that stores a string.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.LongStringPreference
documentation: A preference type that stores a string, but with a textarea widget.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.ChoicePreference

class_variables.name: dynamic_preferences.types.ChoicePreference.choices
documentation:
Expects the same values as for django {py:class}`forms.ChoiceField`.

```
Example

```

```python
class MyChoicePreference(ChoicePreference):
    choices = [
        ('c', 'Carrot'),
        ('t', 'Tomato'),
    ]
```
A preference type that stores a string among a list of choices.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.ModelChoicePreference

class_variables.name: dynamic_preferences.types.ModelChoicePreference.model
documentation:
Which model class to link the preference to. You can skip this if you
define the {py:attr}`queryset` attribute.

class_variables.name: dynamic_preferences.types.ModelChoicePreference.queryset
documentation: A queryset to filter available model instances.

A preference type that stores a reference to a model instance.

```
Example

```

```python
from myapp.blog.models import BlogEntry

@registry.register
class FeaturedEntry(ModelChoicePreference):
    section = Section('blog')
    name = 'featured_entry'
    queryset = BlogEntry.objects.filter(status='published')

blog_entry = BlogEntry.objects.get(pk=12)
manager['blog__featured_entry'] = blog_entry

# accessing the value will return the model instance
assert manager['blog__featured_entry'].pk == 12
```

:::{note}
You should provide either the {py:attr}`queryset` or {py:attr}`model`
attribute
:::

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.ModelMultipleChoicePreference
documentation:
A preference type that stores a reference list to the model instances.

```
Example

```

```python
from myapp.blog.models import BlogEntry

@registry.register
class FeaturedEntries(ModelMultipleChoicePreference):
    section = Section('blog')
    name = 'featured_entries'
    queryset = BlogEntry.objects.all()

blog_entries = BlogEntry.objects.filter(status='published')
manager['blog__featured_entries'] = blog_entries

# accessing the value will return the model queryset
assert manager['blog__featured_entries'] == blog_entries
```

:::{note}
You should provide either the {py:attr}`queryset` or {py:attr}`model`
attribute
:::

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.FilePreference

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.types.FilePreference.serializer
documentation:
The serializer need additional data about the related preference
to upload file to correct directory

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.types.FilePreference.get_file_storage
documentation: Override this method if you want to use a custom storage

A preference type that stores a a reference to a model.

```
Example

```

```python
from django.core.files.uploadedfile import SimpleUploadedFile

@registry.register
class Logo(FilePreference):
    section = Section('blog')
    name = 'logo'

logo = SimpleUploadedFile(
    "logo.png", b"file_content", content_type="image/png")
manager['blog__logo'] = logo

# accessing the value will return a FieldFile object, just as
# django.db.models.FileField
assert manager['blog__logo'].read() == b'file_content'

manager['blog__logo'].delete()
```

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.DurationPreference
documentation: A preference type that stores a timedelta.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.DatePreference
documentation: A preference type that stores a date.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.DateTimePreference
documentation: A preference type that stores a datetime.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.TimePreference
documentation: A preference type that stores a time.

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.types.MultipleChoicePreference
documentation:
A preference type that stores multiple strings among a list of choices.

```
Example

```

```python
@registry.register
class FeaturedEntries(MultipleChoicePreference):
    section = Section('blog')
    name = 'featured_entries'
    choices = [
        ('c', 'Carrot'),
        ('t', 'Tomato'),
    ]
```

:::{note}
Internally, the selected choices are stored as a string, separated by a
separator. The separator defaults to ','. The way this is implemented still
is sae also on keys that cotain the separator, but if in doubt, you can still
set the {py:attr}`separator` to any other character.
:::

classes.name: dynamic_preferences.registries.MissingPreference
documentation:
Used as a fallback when the preference object is not found in registries
This can happen for example when you delete a preference in the code,
but don't remove the corresponding entries in database

Store relationships beetween preferences model and preferences registry

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.registries.PreferenceRegistry.register
documentation:
Store the given preference class in the registry.

```
param preference_class

a  subclass
```

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.registries.PreferenceRegistry._fallback
documentation:
Create a fallback preference object,
This is used when you have model instances that do not match
any registered preferences, see #41

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.registries.PreferenceRegistry.get
documentation:
Returns a previously registered preference

```
param section

The section name under which the preference is registered

type section

str.

param name

The name of the preference. You can use dotted notation 'section.name' if you want to avoid providing section param

type name

str.

param fallback

Should we return a dummy preference object instead of raising an error if no preference is found?

type name

bool.

return

a  instance
```

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.registries.PreferenceRegistry.get_by_name
documentation: Get a preference by name only (no section)

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.registries.PreferenceRegistry.manager
documentation: Return a preference manager that can be used to retrieve preference values

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.registries.PreferenceRegistry.sections
documentation:
---
return: a list of apps with registered preferences
rtype: list
---

methods.name: dynamic_preferences.registries.PreferenceRegistry.preferences
documentation:
Return a list of all registered preferences
or a list of preferences registered for a given section

```
param section

The section name under which the preference is registered

type section

str.

return

a list of  instances
```

Registries are special dictionaries that are used by dynamic-preferences to register and access your preferences.
dynamic-preferences has one registry per Preference type:

- {py:const}`user_preferences`
- {py:const}`site_preferences`
- {py:const}`global_preferences`

In order to register preferences automatically, you must call {py:func}`autodiscover` in your URLconf.

functions.name: dynamic_preferences.management.commands.checkpreferences.delete_preferences
documentation:
Delete preferences objects if they are not present in registry.
Return a list of deleted objects
